domains.name: Domain 1
domains.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When making a psychosocial referral, what are the most important personal characteristics to identify in a mental health caregiver?
choices: Knowledge of the topic, understanding of the sport, and ability to resolve the concern
Knowledge of the type of concern, being open and accepting, and experience in the sport
Willingness to listen, having respect for the patient, and ability to take the patient to task the issue
Being open or receptive, encouraging, and action oriented
Being candid, willing to tell the client what to do, and solution focused

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is an example of a typical psychological construct relevant to sports and athletes and physically active individuals?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: An athlete is returning to practice after a recent injury. During your last session with the athlete, they expressed anxiety and tension about returning. What does the presence of anxiety post-injury predispose the athlete toward?
choices: Higher and lower confidence, depending on the day
Inability to be accepted by the team
General depression and fear of reinjury

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is the primary goal of altruism in athletic training?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which consideration for a psychosocial referral of a patient must be met to ensure the effectiveness of the referral?
choices: Identification of the psychosocial need and the contributing variables

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What are the symptoms of sudden exercise abstinence syndrome (SEAS)?
choices: Cognitive disorders, sleep disorders, and grief
Emotional instability, depression, and chest pain
Behavioral issues, poor digestion, and atrophy

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is a sign or symptom of substance abuse for the physically active?
choices: Having increased physical complaints of unknown origin
Refraining from attending parties where alcohol is served for fear of use

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is a criterion for diagnosis of substance addiction?
choices: The substance is taken in smaller amounts over a shorter period than was intended.
There is a lack of tolerance for the substance, so more of the substance is required to achieve intoxication.
The substance is taken in larger amounts over a longer period than was intended.
The substance use becomes intermittent even when there is a persistent or recurring physical or psychological problem.
Social, occupational, or recreational activities increase because of substance use.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: In the dynamic model of psychological response and sport injury rehabilitation, what does the interaction of responses start with?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which foods have a low glycemic index (less than 55)?
choices: Green leafy vegetables and sweet potato

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which type of social support for the athlete or patient is often overlooked by the athletic trainer?
choices: Informational: educating the athlete or patient about their injury

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which of the following is based on distraction and increasing the patient’s pain threshold?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is an indication that a referral to a mental health professional is recommended?
choices: When aspects of the athlete’s personality require support or intervention beyond that of the athletic trainer
When the athlete is motivated for rehabilitation
When the athlete is pressed for time to perform rehabilitation
When there are cultural differences and practices

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: A patient has a mild form of congenital heart disease. What limitations will this patient have in athletic participation?
choices: To be determined by a cardiologist

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which form of communication is best for interaction because it has the lowest risk of interference?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When releasing the medical information of a patient to a third party, what does HIPAA require that athletic trainers and other covered entities release?
choices: The full medical chart of a patient
The full medical chart plus all electronic communications
A limited amount and frequency of information only for the purpose for which the information is being released
Electronic communications pertaining to the injury
Comments of recovery by coaches and strength staff members

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is an exception to the rule of confidentiality between an athletic trainer and a patient?
choices: When the patient is in clear and imminent danger to themselves or others
When a physician in the stands asks
When the patient is about to be sued for divorce
When the patient is the subject of a media story
When the coach needs to know if the athlete can play

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is part of a records management strategy to reduce risk?
choices: Retain a copy of the architectural drawing of the facility
Document and retain work schedules for five years
Document who was around the patient during treatment
Document the number of bandages used

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What practice do athletic trainers follow to help prevent lawsuits that have components of product liability?
choices: Insist that the athletes read the warning labels on all equipment they use
Obtain written permission to modify equipment
Refer the athlete to a virtual site to learn how to wear equipment
Keep warning labels in a binder

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which state law establishes a certain length of time during which an aggrieved patient may sue a health care provider such as an athletic trainer?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is a legal doctrine that holds that neither the government nor their agents can be held liable for negligent torts?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is a state law that protects health care providers who voluntarily come to the aid of injured persons?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is it called when a patient ignores the prescriptions of their health care provider and it leads to aggravating an injury, thus dividing the liability for an injury between the plaintiff and defendant?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: To prove negligence on the part of the athletic trainer, what is included?
choices: The emergency medical technician staff’s conduct

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: A patient who is 18 years old chooses to participate in athletics against medical advice. Which law, act, or statute gives the patient the legal right to participate?
choices: Family Education Rights and Privacy Act

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How often does the National Athletic Trainers’ Association recommend practicing the emergency action plan?
choices: Once before and once during the sport season
Once a month with various health care providers
At the beginning of each sport season

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is having more job responsibilities and obligations than the time to complete them called?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What relates to a lack of knowledge or skill or both to perform a set of job expectations adequately and acceptably?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When being seen by an athletic trainer, the patient can expect to receive professional behavior and treatment that any similarly trained athletic trainer would provide under the same situation. What is this type of care known as?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is it called when a duty has been breached, resulting in damages to a plaintiff?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How often should equipment with padding and vinyl be cleaned?
choices: Two or three times a week with germicidal cleaner
Once a week with germicidal cleaner
Once every two weeks with germicidal cleaner
Once a month with germicidal cleaner

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Athletic trainers are assigned responsibilities that need to be performed when the team physician is not present. What is the document with these responsibilities called?
choices: In limited circumstances, their job description

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which national standard prohibits an athletic trainer from gambling on an athletic activity in which they have direct influence?
choices: Both the BOC Standards of Professional Practice and NATA Code of Ethics

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: After what period of time can an athletic trainer who has been convicted of a felony apply for reinstatement of membership?
choices: One year after final ruling, court decisions, or completion of sentence or parole
Five years after final ruling, court decisions, or completion of sentence or parole
No change in membership status upon conviction

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which of the following is a sociocultural factor for disordered eating?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: For which of the following sports does NATA neither endorse nor discourage use of helmets or headgear?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How can the distance of a lightning strike be determined if a lightning detector is not available?
choices: Count the seconds from lightning to thunder; seconds equal miles.
Count the seconds from lightning to thunder; divide by five to get miles.
Count the seconds from lightning to thunder; multiply by five to get miles.
Count the seconds from lightning to thunder; divide by three to get miles.
Count the seconds from lightning to thunder; divide by two to get miles.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: A patient asks the athletic trainer to modify a piece of equipment that does not fit properly. What should the athletic trainer do to limit liability?
choices: Should not modify the equipment and should not allow the patient to play with the improperly fitted equipment.
Modify the equipment, educate the patient, and document the modification.
Modify the equipment and have the patient sign a waiver.
Get permission from the team physician and have the athlete sign a waiver.
Get permission from the team physician to modify the equipment.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: In a clinical setting, when can a client be billed for reimbursement?
choices: When the client is doing a home exercise program
When the client comes in early to use equipment but is not supervised
When the athletic trainer is supervising exercise
When the athletic trainer corrects the form of the client doing exercise
When the client is supervised by a lifeguard in the pool

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: To prevent the spread of infectious disease, what type of cleaning agent should be used on wrestling mats?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: An athletic trainer may have been exposed to a blood-borne pathogen. How long must documentation of this exposure be kept?
choices: Three years from date of exposure
Seven years from date of exposure
Three years from end of employment
Seven years from end of employment
Thirty years from date of employment

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Who must be educated regarding infection-control policies and procedures?
choices: The opposing team, team physician, parents, and custodians
The administration, coaches, athletes, and custodians
The administration, coaches, athletes, and opposing athletic trainer
Coaches, athletes, parents, and team physician
Athletes, equipment managers, coaches, and laundry staff

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: In an emergency situation, a 20-year-old person may refuse care. What is the one exception when some care can be given?
choices: When a male family member gives permission
When the patient is intoxicated or under the influence of a drug
When the person is at risk for further injury
When the person appears to be having a mental health crisis

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When a dipstick urinalysis shows the pH is lower than 4.6, what conditions should be suspected to be the reason for this?
choices: Liver disease or a bowel obstruction

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is part of the functional assessment for a concussion?
choices: Any bleeding or bruising of the head
A desire to eat or drink

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is a sign or symptom of an epidural hematoma?
choices: Appearance of being normal when examined

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When, ideally, should baseline balance testing used in concussion screening be performed?
choices: At the end of the previous season
At the start of the competitive schedule
Before the start of the preseason

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What percentage of concussions go unreported until post-practice or post-game?
questions.notes: Correct answer updated per user: e. 85% to 90%.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: If a patient cannot raise their eyebrows or forehead, smile, and frown following a blow to the head, which cranial nerve is involved?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which cranial nerve is responsible for regulating pupil size?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: If a person were to represent themselves as an athletic trainer before passing the BOC certification examination, which national standard has this person violated?
choices: Both the BOC Standards of Professional Practice and NATA Code of Ethics

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When can a multiple adaptor be used safely with electrical modalities?
choices: Only when used with a ground fault interrupter
Only when one modality is being used at a time
Only when the modality has its own fuse
Only when there is an automatic shutoff

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: According to the BOC Standards of Professional Practice, under whose medical direction is an athletic trainer?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: During the preparticipation physical examination (PPE), what is the predictability of future cardiac events if an electrocardiogram is performed?
choices: 90% or better with 12-lead ECG and echocardiogram
Regardless of ECG use, future events not likely to be detected

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What form of mental health therapy works best for a patient who is highly resistant and has low motivation for intervention?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When designing equipment, which factor can help reduce potential injury?
choices: Limitations in the condition of the athlete

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is the purpose of obtaining a baseline neurological screening?
choices: To take part in national injury surveillance systems
To track a patient’s recovery from injury
To track injury recovery times for the national injury database
To provide an opportunity for the patient to get used to the test in the event of neurological injury

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: During the preparticipation physical examination (PPE), a patient is shown to have a vision of 20/10. What does this mean?
choices: The left eye has normal vision at 20 feet, and the right eye has superior vision at 10 feet.
The left eye has normal vision at 20 feet, and the right eye has inferior vision until 10 feet.
The patient can read the letters on the Snellen eye chart from 20 feet away.
The patient can read the letters on the Snellen eye chart on the 20-foot line while standing 20 feet from the chart.
The patient can read the letters on the Snellen eye chart on the 10-foot line while standing 20 feet from the chart.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How often should coaches review films of football games to educate athletes regarding their head contact position?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: A patient with sickle cell trait requires recommendation for preseason training and conditioning. What recommendations or accommodations are necessary to ensure the safety of this patient?
choices: Provide aspirin, increase hydration, and make no modifications to the training program
Provide a gradual progression of exercise and longer recovery time between bouts
Provide the same progressions as for other patients but a longer recovery time between bouts
Provide short bouts of anaerobic exercise for two weeks before sustained exercise
Provide aerobic exercise for two weeks and then move to anaerobic exercise

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of research in injury surveillance?
choices: To monitor the number of injuries that occur among a particular age group
To monitor injuries based on the type of equipment used
To discover injury patterns and be able to modify rules and behaviors to prevent injury
To keep referees, parents, and athletes abreast of injury trends in sport
To determine the injury mechanisms to allow surgeons to improve surgical repair

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What are the training guidelines when the wet-bulb-globe temperature is 65 to 73 °F (18 to 23 °C)?
choices: Risk is moderate but increases as the event progresses.
Athletes need to rest for 10 minutes every hour, drink fluids, and change all wet clothing.
Practice or games should be discontinued.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How long does it take for the cardiovascular system to return to normal in an athlete who has gone from sea level to high altitude?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which football position is most likely to have a cervical spine injury resulting from head-down contact?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: When should the athletic trainer refer a patient for substance abuse?
choices: The patient needs increased amounts of the substance to achieve the desired effect and greater sense of self-awareness.
The patient takes the substance in larger dosages than intended and entices friends to use with them.
The patient feels bad or guilty about substance use and minimizes the amount used.
The patient has a persistent desire but makes unsuccessful efforts to control or cut down substance use and gives up activities because of substance use.
The patient uses the substance early in the morning to improve feelings and to be high functioning.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which characteristic is a likely predictor of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?
choices: Unrealistic expectations about one’s own abilities or a distorted self-image

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Of the following drugs or substances, which predispose an athlete to heat illness?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which sport, if any, can an athlete participate in if they have an acute venous thromboembolism and are taking anticoagulants?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: Which sport, if any, should an athlete with an active case of herpes be disqualified from?
choices: Sports with mats or contact sports until not contagious
No restrictions of any sport as long as the herpes is covered

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: In training room design, it is important to have signage. Which of the following signs need to be in the training room?
choices: Location of ground fault interrupter (GFI) outlets
Location of the private examination room

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: What is the best way to determine the maturation of pediatric patients?

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: How can athletic trainers assist in reducing the risk of overuse injuries in youth?
choices: Encourage youth to participate in a variety of sports.
Increase activity intensity at younger ages.
Encourage children to push their competitiveness at younger ages.

questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.prompt: An athlete with Down syndrome wishes to participate in soccer. What associated medical condition should be ruled out prior to participation?

questions.title: Testlet 1
questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. A 16-year-old football player stumbles to the sideline after being tackled. The player complains of dizziness and light-headedness. During your assessment and possible referral, what are the following considerations?

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer needs to determine the possibility of a concussion in a player. When using the Sport Concussion Assessment Tool 6 (SCAT6), in what order should the athletic trainer perform the actions noted to determine the level of the injury? Drag the list items into the correct order.
questions.supporting_text: Place the SCAT6 assessment steps in the correct order.

questions.prompt: Of the following, which are the cognitive signs or symptoms of an acute concussion in an athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Slurred speech, retrograde amnesia, and irritability
Headache, vacant stare, and delayed verbal responses

questions.prompt: Which of the following are part of the functional assessment for a concussion in an athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Any bleeding or bruising of the head

questions.prompt: When using the Balance Error Scoring System (BESS) Test with this athlete, which are the three phases that should be performed in proper sequence?
questions.supporting_text: Choose the three BESS phases and place them in the correct order.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer needs to determine if this athlete has predisposing risk factors for a concussion. Which are the variables that are predisposing risk factors for a concussion? Choose all that apply.

One of the concerns for an athlete returning with a concussion is second-impact syndrome. Which of the following actions should be taken if the player exhibits second-impact syndrome? Choose all that apply.
choices: Place the patient in a quiet environment and lower the lights, then refer the patient to a physician.
Have the patient sit out of practice and games until he can pass the concussion testing standards for return to play.
Test the patient’s level of consciousness, check reflexes, and check blood pressure and pulses.
Call 9-1-1, monitor vital signs, and begin CPR as needed.
Look for obvious deformities and for increased pain and place ice on the area of impact.
Test to reset a baseline for future concussion injuries.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer needs to determine whether a referral is needed. Which symptoms would indicate the need for an immediate referral, rather than a delayed referral? Choose all that apply.
The athletic trainer needs to determine which cranial nerve(s) have been impacted. Which special test matches the cranial nerve that has been impacted?

pairs.right: Ask the athlete to stand on one foot
Ask the athlete to say Ahhh
Ask the athlete to stick out their tongue

questions.prompt: What advice should be given to the athlete and adult supervisor (e.g., parent, guardian) that would provide guidance until the athlete is seen the next day? Choose all that apply.
choices: Take sleeping pills if they find it difficult to fall asleep.
If double vision is reported, take the person to an emergency room.
For a worsening headache, use aspirin.

questions.prompt: What is the correct order of stages of return from concussion following the cessation of symptoms and return to baseline neurocognitive testing?
questions.supporting_text: Place the return-to-play stages in the correct order after concussion symptoms resolve and neurocognitive testing returns to baseline.

questions.prompt: What criteria and increments should be used to ensure a safe return to participation post-concussion? Choose all that apply.
choices: When everything is normal, resume all activities.
When pre-injury baselines are normal, increase activity, starting with cardiovascular and then sport-specific activities.
When pre-injury baselines are normal, increase activity, starting with strength training, moving to cardiovascular, and then moving to sport-specific activities.
When pre-injury baselines are normal, increase activity, starting with anaerobic activities, followed by endurance activities and then sport-specific activities and impact activities.
When pre-injury baselines are normal, increase activity, starting with cardiovascular activities and then moving to impact injuries that emulate how the athlete became injured.

questions.title: Testlet 2
questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. A 19-year-old woman soccer player reports to an athletic trainer that she has been feeling down and anxious for several months. What are the considerations taken into account in deciding to make a referral to a mental health care professional for this athlete?

questions.prompt: When speaking with the athlete about their concern, which of the following would be an example of a typical psychological construct relevant to sports and athletes or physically active individuals? Choose all that apply.

Paraphrasing is a skill that lets the soccer player know the athletic trainer has really heard what the individual has said when taking down a history of their mental concerns. Which are the specific steps in paraphrasing? Choose all that apply.
choices: Rephrase concisely the key words and concepts of the patient to reword what has been spoken in fresh or different words.
Identify the content of the message to decide what the patient is talking about.
Recall the patient’s message to make sure you have heard it.
Parrot back what has been said.
Include a perception check that allows the patient to agree or disagree with you.

questions.prompt: What is an indication that a referral to a mental health professional is recommended for this soccer player? Choose all that apply.
choices: When aspects of the athlete’s personality require support or intervention beyond that of the athletic trainer
When the athlete is motivated for rehabilitation
When cultural differences and practices exist between the athlete and the athletic trainer
When the athlete is having a hard time adjusting to the team
When the athletic trainer feels bias concerning this athlete is compromising the athletic trainer’s ability to work positively to resolve the situation

questions.prompt: When considering a psychosocial referral of the soccer player, which of the following considerations need to be met to ensure the effectiveness of the referral? Choose all that apply.
choices: Identification of the psychosocial need and the contributing variables

questions.prompt: When considering a psychosocial referral for the soccer player, what are the most important personal characteristics to identify in a mental health caregiver? Choose all that apply.
choices: Availability for counseling sessions with the soccer player

questions.prompt: The discussion with the soccer player’s mental health concern should be treated in an ethical manner. Which options indicate unethical conduct that the athletic trainer must avoid to refer this soccer player for care in an ethical manner? Choose all that apply.
choices: Conflict of interest in reporting to the coach as part of the coaching staff
Exploiting the relationship with the soccer player by dating or coercing the soccer player to return before being healed
Clear boundaries of personal relationship between the soccer player and the athletic trainer

questions.title: Testlet 3
questions.category: Risk Reduction, Wellness, and Health Literacy
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. A football player who is being highly recruited to play Division I sustains a blow to their upper thorax during a Friday night game. The athletic trainer assesses the injury as a chest contusion and permits the athlete to return to the game late in the fourth quarter. The athletic trainer tells the football player that if his condition gets worse, he is to call the athletic trainer or go to the emergency room. However, after going home, the football player reports to his mother that his chest continues to hurt. His mother tells him to “tough it out” and try to get some sleep. The athlete continues to have pain in his left rib cage, and in the middle of the night, his left shoulder starts to hurt. The athlete decides to wait until morning to tell his mother. When he doesn’t respond to his mother’s call to breakfast, she goes into his room and finds him unresponsive and in shock. The mother calls for an ambulance, and the player is then rushed to the hospital. After having his spleen removed and spending six days in the hospital, two of which were in intensive care following the surgery, the player is released to go home. The parents are considering a lawsuit against the athletic trainer. What are the legal considerations for the athletic trainer?

questions.prompt: The mother is considering a lawsuit. Match the legal definitions in a lawsuit to the proper term:

pairs.right: It is a state law that sets a certain length of time during which an aggrieved person may sue a health care provider.
Neither the government nor their agents (athletic trainer) can be held liable for an act.
The football player was aware of the risks of participation and decided to proceed anyway.
A health care provider voluntarily comes to the aid of an injured person.
A patient ignores the prescription of their health care provider, a disregard that can lead to an injury or an aggravation of an injury.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer is anticipating a lawsuit and has already purchased liability malpractice insurance. What should the athletic trainer think about when considering purchasing professional liability insurance? Choose all that apply.
choices: Exemptions in the policy, such as sexual assault or substance abuse
Whether the policy identifies or restricts attorneys for defense

questions.prompt: The football player ignores the instructions of the athletic trainer to report worsening symptoms upon returning home after the game, which led to a delay in care of the injury, dividing the liability for an injury between the plaintiff (the mother and the football player) and the defendant (the athletic trainer and the high school). The athletic trainer is also employed by a governmental agency. Which of the following describes this scenario? Choose all that apply.
To prove negligence on the part of the athletic trainer, which factors are taken into consideration? Choose all that apply.
choices: Conduct by the opposing player who hit the football player in the chest
Existence of and breach of duty

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer is held to a set of legal standards. Match the legal terms with its definition.

pairs.right: The boundaries within which an athletic trainer may provide their duties
The level at which a reasonable athletic trainer would practice
The failure to act as a reasonable athletic trainer would act in the same circumstance
A responsibility to provide care based upon a relationship between the athletic trainer and their patient
Finding that the athletic trainer had an obligation owed to a harmed patient and that it was violated
Getting a patient to understand what occurs and the risks and benefits of an actual treatment and getting their permission to treat based on the information

questions.prompt: Which of the following describes when a duty for care by the athletic trainer has been breached, resulting in damages to the football player?
The mother’s attorney wants to know if the athletic trainer was certified and licensed to practice as an athletic trainer in the state. What is the purpose of certification and licensure?
choices: It gives status to athletic trainers.
It proves athletic trainers have basic skills.
Athletic trainers can charge for services.
Athletic trainers can render health care.

questions.prompt: You are seeing a patient who has complaints of elbow pain and swelling. The patient works a desk job and spends a lot of time resting their elbows on the armrest of their desk chair. You believe this has caused the swelling seen on the elbow pictured. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?
What possible injury is indicated by an inability to pronate and supinate the forearm with point tenderness over the radial head?
An athlete fell on an outstretched arm during practice and has dislocated their elbow. What is the most common direction of displacement in an elbow (humeroulnar joint) dislocation?
When doing the radioulnar joint stress test, which ligament is examined for pain and laxity?
What is the normal carrying angle of the elbow?
A patient has noticed a gradual onset of pain at the medial aspect of the wrist. It is most notable during lifting or when grasping heavy items. Which special test is most likely to be positive?
Which of the carpal bones is most commonly fractured?
Which special test is pictured?
Which nerve is involved in carpal tunnel syndrome?
What is the percentage, on average, that someone is in the stance phase of the walking gait compared to the swing phase of gait?
What instrument can the athletic trainer use to assess pulmonary function?
How is asthma defined?
choices: Blood accumulating in the pleural space
A reversible airway obstruction that results from hyperreactivity
A respiratory condition leading to a residual cardiac condition

questions.prompt: What is the underlying physiological mechanism of sickle cell anemia?
choices: A decrease in circulating red blood cells
A relative increase in blood plasma volume

questions.prompt: What valve is an athletic trainer listening to when performing cardiac auscultation within the right second intercostal space at the right sternal border?
What valve is an athletic trainer listening to when performing cardiac auscultation within the fourth intercostal space along the lower left sternal border?
Which of the following ear-related conditions does this image indicate?
What condition is shown in this figure?
What part of the eye is composed of a dense white connective tissue that makes up 90% of the outer layer of the eyeball globe?

Three athletes present in the athletic training facility with complaints of diarrhea, vomiting, nausea, and abdominal cramping one to three hours after eating at a local restaurant. What may be the problem?

A patient presents to the athletic trainer in the early morning, before class, with an eye that looks like that in the photo. They complain that their eye is itchy and painful with “disgusting yellow discharge on the pillow.” They are also complaining of blurred vision. What condition could the patient have?
What is poliomyelitis?
choices: An infection by a polio-parasitic infection that attacks motor neurons in the spinal cord
An infection by the poliovirus that attacks motor neurons in the spinal cord
An infection by the polio bacterium that attacks motor neurons in the spinal cord
An infection by the polio bacterium that attacks sensory neurons in the spinal cord
An infection by the poliovirus that attacks sensory neurons in the spinal cord

questions.prompt: Which of the following conditions is associated with disproportionate pain, extreme skin sensitivity, a burning sensation, and hyperhidrosis?
Which of the following consequences may occur after a prolonged bout of diarrhea (24 to 36 hours)?
Which condition can lead to congenital defects or death to infants if the mother becomes infected within the first trimester?
What is the underlying pathophysiology of type 2 non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus?
choices: Inability to produce insulin receptor sites

questions.prompt: Which definition below best describes sports hematuria?
choices: An indication that something is wrong with the kidneys
Benign self-limiting blood in the urine associated with activity and no associated trauma
A lack of a menstrual cycle due to excessive sport activity
Occult blood in the urine indicating potentially serious kidney damage associated with sports
Something found in a high percentage of marathon or long-distance runners

questions.prompt: A patient complains to an athletic trainer of unplanned weight loss, polyuria, and polydipsia. The team physician suspects the patient may be diabetic and orders a blood test. What would confirm the physician’s suspicion this person is a diabetic?
Which of the following diseases is malignant?
Which term best describes the end-feel created when two muscles come in contact with each other?
Which of the following refers to the process by which a patient’s condition is defined through a progressive exclusion of conditions that are inconsistent with the signs and symptoms presented?
Which of the following refers to chronic irritation and excess secretion of synovial fluid within the joint capsule?
Over which of the following anatomic structures would tightness of the iliotibial band create excessive friction?
Besides performing a valgus and varus knee stress test with the knee straight, at what joint angle should the knee be stressed in a flexed position?
Which of the following conditions is associated with a predisposition for patella subluxation?
What test is used to differentiate between the tightness of the iliopsoas muscle and the tightness of the rectus femoris muscle?

You are evaluating a 13-year-old boy who presents with pain at the patellar tendon. He reports pain especially when jumping or running. Upon observation, you notice a large bump at the tibial tuberosity. Which condition would the patient most likely present with?

External tibial rotation plus valgus force
Internal tibial rotation plus valgus force
External tibial rotation plus varus force
Internal tibial rotation plus anterior force

questions.prompt: Which of the following terms is defined as a lower-extremity alignment that is commonly referred to as “bow-legged”?
Which of the following terms properly designates the foot alignment and structure associated with a navicular tuberosity location that is above the Feiss line in a weight-bearing condition?

When the sternoclavicular joint is dislocated, which direction of clavicle displacement can present a medical emergency?

What is her diagnosis?

questions.title: Testlet 1
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. An outfielder on the softball team (20 years old, 5’5”, 140 lb) comes to the athletic training facility complaining of pain in their right knee. The pain has been progressing for the last one or two months and is starting to affect their ability to practice and move without pain. The athlete also reports a couple of “giving-way” episodes over the last two months. The athlete is currently in-season.

questions.prompt: What questions does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding a history for this injured athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Have you ever injured this knee previously?
Do you recall how you initially injured this knee?
What did you do this past weekend?
Does your pain radiate up or down the leg?
Do you have a family history of knee injuries?
How would you describe the pain in your knee?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain?

questions.prompt: What additional questions does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding a history for this injured athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Have you been treating this injury yourself? And if so, what have you been doing for it?
Have you considered playing a different position? What makes the injured body part feel worse?
Is there a time of the day that your knee feels better?
Is there a time of the day that your knee feels worse?

questions.prompt: About two months ago, they twisted the knee sliding into second base, and the pain level is a 4 on a 10-point scale. The athlete reveals they have been taking Advil a couple of times per day, and the knee has a dull achy pain that begins 20 minutes after moving around a bit. The athletic trainer has elected to measure the patient’s active and passive range of motion. What are the appropriate movements that the athletic trainer should assess? Choose all that apply.
choices: Active internal rotation of the tibia
Active external rotation of the tibia

questions.prompt: Read the patient evaluation. Next, the athletic trainer moves to assessing the strength using manual muscle testing. Drag the primary muscles being tested into the column for the muscles being tested.
questions.supporting_text:
The patient evaluation reveals the following:
       1. Knee flexion: −4 on the involved side, 5+ on the uninvolved side
       2. Knee extension: 4 on the involved side, 5 on the uninvolved side
       3. Hip abduction: −4 on the involved side, +4 on the uninvolved side
       4. Hip extension with the knee bent to 90 degrees: 5 bilaterally

zones.label: Hip abduction

questions.prompt: Both the neurological and circulatory assessments were within normal limits. Which are the special tests that may be most appropriate to aid in the assessment of this athlete?
Both the neurological and circulatory assessments were within normal limits. Which are the special tests that may be most appropriate to aid in the assessment of this athlete?
Both the neurological and circulatory assessments were within normal limits. Which are the special tests that may be most appropriate to aid in the assessment of this athlete?
Read the patient evaluation. Which injuries or conditions are a part of the differential diagnosis based on the findings? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The examination reveals the following:
  - Anterior drawer test: Patient is guarding during the test—appears to have some laxity but is unsure of endpoint.
  - Ballotable patella: Mild effusion is present.
  - Bounce home: Pain is elicited at full knee extension.
  - Clarke’s sign/patellar grind: It is painful on the medial side of the knee during the test.
  - Lachman test: Patient is guarding during the test—appears to have some laxity but is unsure of endpoint.
  - Prone Lachman test: Patient is guarding during the test—appears to have some laxity but is unsure of endpoint.
  - Wilson’s test: Pain is at approximately 30°—relieved with external rotation of the tibia.
  - Pivot shift test: Patient is guarding during the test—unsure if laxity is present.
choices: Contusion of the infrapatellar fat pad

questions.title: Testlet 2
questions.scenario:
Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. A high school athlete comes into the training facility after that afternoon’s lacrosse practice complaining of left wrist pain. He was shoved from behind into the bleachers during physical education class while playing floor hockey. The athlete indicates he had immediate pain, but that the pain has subsided. Currently, he describes the pain as more of a dull ache. Upon inspection of the area, the athletic trainer notes the following:
  - Mild swelling over the posterior radial aspect of the wrist
  - Pain over the area that is swollen
  - No obvious fractures or dislocations

questions.prompt: What questions does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding a history for this injured athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Have you ever injured this wrist previously?
What makes the injured body part feel worse?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain?
How did it go at practice? Were you able to do much?
How would you describe the pain in your wrist?
Does your pain radiate up your arm or down into your hand?

questions.prompt: Next the athletic trainer palpates the wrist. What are the anatomical landmarks with the corresponding letter?
questions.supporting_text:
The history of this patient reveals the following:
  - What is the pain level? ("Currently it is about 6 on a 10-point scale. When I try to move it after leaving it still for a bit, I get a really sharp pain that would be about an 8.")
  - What makes the injured body part feel better? ("Doing nothing! When I was in class, as long as I had my hand and arm on the desk, it was not painful. But when I tried to lift it off the desk, it was really sore.")
  - What makes the injured body part feel worse? ("Movement; any time I move the wrist it is painful.")
  - How would you describe the pain? ("A dull, achy pain with occasional sharp pain when I move the wrist.")
  - Does your pain radiate up your arm or down into your hand? ("No, it only hurts where I told you it bothered me.")
  - How would you describe the pain in your wrist? ("A dull, achy pain with occasional sharp pain when I move the wrist.")
  - How did it go at practice? Were you able to do much? ("Practice did not go well. I tried to stay out of as many drills as possible, but Coach got upset at me because I was not taking my turn in the rotation.")

questions.title: Testlet 3
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. A 19-year-old female field hockey player sees the athletic trainer early one morning in the athletic training facility. She complains of feeling really tired. She tells the athletic trainer that she feels like she cannot get enough sleep. Her additional history is as follows: She has a history of headaches, but denies having one today. She could not get warm yesterday, but today her head feels hot. She has a sore throat and still has her tonsils. She has seasonal allergies, an allergy to sulfa drugs, and shellfish. She takes Tylenol when she has a headache. She tried to eat a hamburger last night but vomited and does not feel like eating today. She took care of her boyfriend last week, and he had similar symptoms. She collided with another player last week and was sore for a day. Her abdomen is a little sore.

questions.prompt: As the athletic trainer conducts the history portion of the examination, they are also incorporating several special tests. Which tests would be most appropriate for the athletic trainer’s patient based on the information gathered at this point? Choose all that apply.
choices: Otoscope of ears, nose, and throat

questions.prompt: Review the test findings and then drag the organs listed to their proper abdominal quadrant.
questions.supporting_text:
The special test performed revealed the following things:
     -Auscultation of the abdomen and thoracic cavity is normal.
     -Examination of the nose and ears is within normal limits.
     -Examination of the throat reveals that it is red with white exudate, and the tonsils are swollen.
The athletic trainer does palpation.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer finds both right quadrants and the lower left quadrant to be pain-free and within normal limits. There is concern about the possibility of an enlargement of organ tissue in the upper left quadrant. What examinations or assessment should the athletic trainer do at this point? Choose all that apply.
choices: Take the patient’s blood glucose level
Request a urine sample from the patient

questions.prompt: Place these three tests in the correct order.
questions.supporting_text:
The examination reveals the following information:
     -The body temperature is 100.2 °F.
     -There is a dullness over solid organs in the abdomen.
     -Percussion of the thoracic cavity is normal.
     -The pulse rate is 88 and respirations are 12 per minute.
     -The patient’s blood pressure is slightly higher than normal
There is a specific order in which percussion, palpation, and auscultation are to be performed. What is the order for these three special tests as they pertain to an abdominal examination?

questions.prompt: What is the athletic trainer’s differential diagnosis of this athlete’s injury? What are those injuries or conditions that are a result of the examination? Choose all that apply.

questions.title: Testlet 4
questions.scenario: Please use the following information to answer the questions in this testlet. It is preseason camp for a small college men’s soccer team. Right after lunch, two soccer players bring a third soccer player to the athletic training facility. The two friends tell the athletic trainer that there is something wrong with their teammate. They tell the athletic trainer that Joe (the injured player) was “talking nonsense during lunch” and “did not look or act right.” They voice their concern about Joe and state they want to have Joe examined by the athletic trainer.

questions.prompt: What questions does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding a history for this injured athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Can you tell me what happened?
Have you ever hurt your head previously?
Do you have pain anywhere on your head?
Are you having any other symptoms I should be aware of?
Why did you continue to play after you hit your head?

questions.prompt: What additional questions does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding a history for this injured athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Did you lose consciousness or black out?
Does your neck bother you at all?
Do you have any numbness and tingling down your arms or legs?
What type of pain are you experiencing? In other words, is it a sharp pain? A dull ache? A throbbing pain?
On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your pain?
Does your pain come and go or is it fairly constant?

questions.prompt: Read the patient history. Which of the following are some concussion-related symptoms asked of the patient during head injury assessment? Choose all that apply.
questions.review_notes: Image/photo appears required but no image ID was identified in source.
questions.supporting_text:
The history reveals the following:

  - The patient was doing heading drills for about 20 minutes.
  - The patient’s head collided with another player on the right side of his head, and he has consistent throbbing pain.
  - His pain level is 8 on a 10-point scale.
  - His neck feels stiff, but he has no pain or numbness anywhere else.
  - The patient takes Adderall for ADHD.

questions.prompt: Read the concussion evaluation. The athletic trainer inspects the head, face, and neck. Which of the following are the specific features the athletic trainer needs to inspect for when conducting an evaluation of head and face? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The patient reveals the following information from the concussion evaluation:
     -The patient has light sensitivity and a stiff and sore neck.
     -He has a headache and is having difficulty in remembering things.
questions.review_notes: Structured item; review drop spots/pairs during HTML conversion. Source labels this as drag-and-drop but includes answer options; preserve source type and review interaction type.
questions.json_conversion_notes: Create JSON dd object with tokens and zones.
choices: Discoloration behind the eyes, known as “racoon eyes”
Discoloration behind the ear at the mastoid process, known as a “Battle’s Sign”
Bleeding around the gumline of the teeth
Fluid coming from the eye, known as opthorrhea
Fluid coming from ears, known as otorrhea
Fluid coming from the nostrils, known as rhinorrhea
Discoloration around the neck and throat region

questions.prompt: Read the patient’s report. What is the total Standardized Assessment of Concussion (SAC) score earned by this patient? Choose only one.
questions.supporting_text:
From the previous questions, the athletic trainer discovers that the patient’s pupils are equal and react to light, and the Romberg test shows mild instability when progressing to eyes closed and single-leg position.
Next, the athletic trainer performs a standard assessment of concussion. The patient’s responses to each segment are below:
ORIENTATION:
Month (August)
Date (The 26th)
Day of week (Wednesday)
Year (2026)
Time (within 1 hour) (Maybe 20 minutes until 2)
IMMEDIATE MEMORY: [Note: (+) = a correct response; (―) = an incorrect response]
Word Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Elbow + + +
Apple + + +
Carpet — + —
Saddle + — +
Bubble — + —
CONCENTRATION: (Note: BOLDED and Highlighted strings were correctly responded to by the patient, strings with a strikethrough were incorrectly responded to, and strings in normal font were not used for this patient.)
4-9-3 6-2-9
3-8-1-4 3-2-7-9
6-2-9-7-1 1-5-2-8-5
7-1-8-4-6-2 5-3-9-1-4-8
Months of the year, backward (Note: BOLDED and Highlighted strings were correctly responded to by the patient, strings with a strikethrough line were incorrectly responded to)
December - November - October - September - August - July - June - May - April - March - February - January
Neurological assessment of cervical vertebrae dermatome, myotomes and reflexes are all within normal limits.
DELAYED RECALL:
Elbow +
Apple +
Carpet +
Saddle +
Bubble —
20/30
24/30
questions.supporting_html:
From the previous questions, the athletic trainer discovers that the patient’s pupils are equal and react to light, and the Romberg test shows mild instability when progressing to eyes closed and single-leg position.

Next, the athletic trainer performs a standard assessment of concussion. The patient’s responses to each segment are below:

ORIENTATION:

Month(August)
Date(The 26th)
Day of week(Wednesday)
Year(2026)
Time (within 1 hour)(Maybe 20 minutes until 2)

IMMEDIATE MEMORY: [Note: (+) = a correct response; (―) = an incorrect response]

WordTrial 1Trial 2Trial 3
Elbow+++
Apple+++
Carpet―+―
Saddle+―+
Bubble―+―

CONCENTRATION: (Note: BOLDED and Highlighted strings were correctly responded to by the patient, strings with a strikethrough were incorrectly responded to, and strings in normal font were not used for this patient.)

4-9-3   6-2-9

3-8-1-4   3-2-7-9

6-2-9-7-1   1-5-2-8-5

7-1-8-4-6-2   5-3-9-1-4-8

Months of the year, backward (Note: BOLDED and Highlighted strings were correctly responded to by the patient, strings with a strikethrough line were incorrectly responded to)

December - November - October - September - August - July - June - May - April - March - February - January

Neurological assessment of cervical vertebrae dermatome, myotomes and reflexes are all within normal limits.

DELAYED RECALL:

Elbow+
Apple+
Carpet+
Saddle+
Bubble―

questions.prompt: A balance error scoring system (BESS) is performed next. Which of the following are considered errors for the BESS? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The patient reveals the following information from the concussion evaluation:
     -The patient has light sensitivity and a stiff and sore neck.
     -He has a headache and is having difficulty in remembering things.
choices: Rocking back and forth or side to side
Lifting the hands off the iliac crests
Stepping, stumbling, or falling out of the stance
Flexing or abducting the hip more than 30°
Moving the neck in any direction for more than 15°
Remaining out of the stance for more than five seconds
Flexing the knee of the stance leg(s) more than 25°

questions.prompt: Which of the following special tests might the athletic trainer use to further assess this patient’s dynamic balance, concentration, and coordination? Choose all that apply.
Read the scenario. What warnings should the athletic trainer share with this patient’s mother? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text: As the athletic trainer was finishing this assessment, a woman walks in, and the patient recognizes her as his mother. The athletic trainer informs the mother that in all likelihood her son has suffered a concussion. The athletic trainer assures the mother there is nothing overly serious happening. However, the athletic trainer does want to give a list of potential red flags she needs to be on the lookout for during the next several hours.
choices: Any alteration in the level of consciousness
Fixed or abnormal pupil reactions, abnormal eye movements, or acute visual impairments
Any focal neurological symptoms occurring after head trauma
Paralysis or progressive (either unilateral or bilateral) muscle weakness
Acute severe headache, especially associated with nausea and vomiting or focal neurological deficits
Constant repeating of questions about self or past events

metadata.note: Updated from the verified Domain 2 Excel spreadsheet. Domain 2 testlet scenarios are stored once at the testlet level. Image placeholder objects were added for image-needed rows.
